solution.p: We will factor t^{2}+10t+16 where a=1, b=10, and c=16.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For t^{2}+10t+16, since the product of 2 and 8 is 16, and the sum of 2 and 8 is 10, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+10t+16\amp =\left(t+2\right)\mathopen{}\left(t+8\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (16) = 16.

The number 16 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)
ewline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 10, and this sum happens with 8+2=10 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+10t+16\amp =t^{2}+8t+2t+16
ewline \amp =t\mathopen{}\left(t+8ight)+2\mathopen{}\left(t+8ight)
ewline \amp =\left(t+2ight)\mathopen{}\left(t+8ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in t^{2}+10t+16 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 16, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)\newline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with t^{2}+10t+16 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+10t+16\amp =\left(t+2\right)\mathopen{}\left(t+8\right)\end{aligned}
